perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information disclosed to Receiving Party by any Disclosing Party, its related entities and/or agents is covered by this Agreement. C onfidential Information shall not include any information that: (i) is or subsequently becomes publicly available without Receiving Party’s breach of any obligation owed to Disclosing Party; (ii) became known to Receiving Party prior to Disclosing Party’s disclosure of such information to Receiving Party; (iii) became known to Receiving Party from a source other than Disclosing Party other than by the breach of an obligation of confidentiali ty owed to Disclosing Party; (iv) is independently developed by Rec eiving Party without access to the Disclosing Party’s information , or (v ) the Confidential Information is required to be disclosed pursuant to a requirement of a governmental agency or law so long as the other party is provided notice of such requirement p rior to any such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information disclosed to Receiving Party by any Disclosing Party, its related entities and/or agents is covered by this Agreement. Confidential Information shall not include any information that: (i) is or subsequently becomes publicly available, regardless of Receiving Party’s actions; (ii) became known to Receiving Party at any time; (iii) became known to Receiving Party from any source; (iv) is independently developed by Receiving Party; or (v) the Confidential Information is required to be disclosed pursuant to a requirement of a governmental agency or law even without prior notice to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text broadens the exceptions to 'Confidential Information,' removing the requirement that the information becoming publicly available must be without the Receiving Party's breach. It also removes the requirement that prior knowledge of information must occur 'prior' to disclosure by the other party. Furthermore, it removes the condition that the source of knowledge cannot breach confidentiality. It also eliminates the notice requirement regarding disclosure mandated by law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the MUTSA's definition of trade secrets, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy and provides protection against misappropriation. By broadly excluding information regardless of how it becomes public, the contract undermines the very essence of trade secret protection as defined by Massachusetts law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text significantly weakens the protection of confidential information, contradicting the principles of trade secret law as described in the provided snippets. Specifically, the removal of conditions related to how information becomes publicly available, how the receiving party obtained prior knowledge, and the elimination of notice requirements for legally mandated disclosures directly undermine the 'duty to limit acquisition, disclosure, or use of information' as mentioned in both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, which are derived from Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93, Section 42. By broadening the exceptions to what constitutes confidential information, the contract fails to reasonably protect trade secrets from misappropriation, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that it shall not make use of, disseminate, or in any way disclose any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to any person, firm, or business, except to the extent necessary for the T ransaction.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that it shall not make use of, disseminate, or in any way disclose any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to any person, firm, or business, with no exceptions.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'except to the extent necessary for the Transaction' clause, the modified text creates an impossibly strict obligation. This conflicts with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, which is a part of every contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing under Massachusetts Law
perturbation.law_citation: Unofficial, but derived from legal precedent.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. This means that parties must act honestly and reasonably in performing their contractual obligations. Eliminating any possibility of using confidential information even when necessary for the transaction creates an unworkable and unreasonable constraint, potentially violating this implied duty. The first URL explains Good Faith. The second URL is a case showing Good Faith in Massachusetts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the exception allowing the use of confidential information 'to the extent necessary for the Transaction'. This creates an absolute prohibition, which the Law Explanation argues violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'good faith' as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'observance of fair dealing standards'. By removing the 'necessary for the Transaction' clause, the modified contract imposes an unreasonable constraint that could be seen as a failure to act in 'good faith', as it eliminates any flexibility, even when necessary for the transaction to occur. Therefore, the change introduces a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Both parties acknowledge that the Receiving Party shall protect the secrecy of all Confidential Information, that said Confidential Information is of critical importance to the Disclosing Party, that any violation of this Agreement would seriously and irreparably impair and damage the Disclosing Party's business, and that the Recipient shall keep all Confidential Information in a fiduciary capacity for the sole benefit of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Both parties acknowledge that the Receiving Party shall protect the secrecy of all Confidential Information, that said Confidential Information is of critical importance to the Disclosing Party, that any violation of this Agreement would seriously and irreparably impair and damage the Disclosing Party's business, and that the Recipient shall keep all Confidential Information with due diligence.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'fiduciary capacity' with 'due diligence,' the modified text weakens the Receiving Party's obligation. A fiduciary duty is a much higher standard of care than due diligence.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text asserts that confidential information should be maintained in a 'fiduciary capacity'. The term 'fiduciary duty' has precise legal meaning, implying a higher standard of care. By changing this to 'due diligence' the contract attempts to lower the standard to a reasonable level of care. The first URL leads to Cornell Law School's definition of Fiduciary and the second to CFI's definition.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: fiduciary | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you fiduciary A fiduciary, derived from the Latin term for “trust”, is a person owing a fiduciary duty to another. When someone has a fiduciary duty to someone else, the person with the duty must act in a way that will benefit someone else financially. Owing a fiduciary duty to a party creates a fiduciary relationship . perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Fiduciary Duty - Definition, Examples of Fiduciary Relationships Home › Resources › Wealth Management › Fiduciary Duty Table of Contents What is Fiduciary Duty? What is a Fiduciary? Examples of Fiduciary Relationships Fiduciary Duty – An Obligation of Respect Related Readings Fiduciary Duty The act of overseeing the wealth of clients, acting on the client’s behalf and in their best interests Written by CFI Team Read Time 2 minutes What is Fiduciary Duty? Fiduciary duty is the responsibility that fiduciaries are tasked with when dealing with other parties, specifically in relation to financial matters . In most cases, it means that the duties involve a fiduciary overseeing the wealth of their clients, acting on the client’s behalf, and in their best interests. What is a Fiduciary? To better understand fiduciary duty, it’s first important to know what a fiduciary is. A fiduciary holds ethical and legal responsibilities to his clients – a relationship that requires trust and prudence on the part of the fiduciary. Clients often entrust a significant amount of wealth to a fiduciary, whether it’s in the form of cash or other assets . Part of the fiduciary’s role is to act, always and first and foremost, in the best interest of his clients. In most cases, fiduciaries should not reap any direct financial gain from any of the decisions they make or the actions they take on behalf of their clients. If they do, it should be only with full consent from the client. Examples of Fiduciary Relationships A fiduciary relationship is the one between the fiduciary and the beneficiary or client. Some examples of fiduciary relationships are listed below: Brokers and principals Trustees and beneficiaries Attorneys and clients Not all fiduciary roles are served in relation to financial obligations. An attorney , for example, may or may not be caring for an individual’s financial assets when acting in their fiduciary capacity. In every type of fiduciary/client relationship, it’s the fiduciary’s duty to act in a way that best serves his client. That means not entering into any other relationships that would result in a conflict of interest , making a plan of action with the client to ensure the client’s desired outcome is achieved, keeping the client updated on any happenings and/or changes, and being available to answer questions or resolve issues as they arise. A classic example of violating one’s fiduciary duty would be a financial advisor who steers a client toward making investment “A” even though the fiduciary is aware that investment “B” is more likely to generate the best return on investment – simply because the fiduciary will earn a large commission from convincing the client to invest in investment A. In such a scenario, the fiduciary is putting their own best interest ahead of the best interest of their client. Fiduciary Duty – An Obligation of Respect Fiduciary duty is one of the highest and most respected obligations. Fiduciaries are paid to work. However, they are also tasked with the responsibility of being the eyes and ears of their client, working to secure the client’s desired outcomes without any promise of additional compensation for doing so. perturbation.justification: The original text stipulates that the Receiving Party must handle Confidential Information in a 'fiduciary capacity,' which, according to Scraped Law Snippet 1, is derived from the Latin term for “trust” and means the person with the duty must act in a way that will benefit someone else financially.. By changing this to 'due diligence,' the modified text reduces the standard of care required. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that a fiduciary holds ethical and legal responsibilities to his clients – a relationship that requires trust and prudence on the part of the fiduciary, and that a fiduciary duty is one of the highest and most respected obligations. Therefore, the change contradicts the higher standard imposed by fiduciary duty.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confident ial Information and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies, to such injunctive and other equitable relief (without bond and without the necessity of showing actual monetary damages) as may be deemed proper by a court.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled, waiving all other rights or remedies, to such injunctive and other equitable relief (with bond and with the necessity of showing actual monetary damages) as may be deemed proper by a court.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text forces the Disclosing Party to waive all other rights or remedies (besides injunctive relief). It also requires a bond and the showing of actual monetary damages for injunctive relief.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Principles of Contract Law Regarding Remedies
perturbation.law_explanation: Contract law generally provides for a range of remedies for breach of contract, including monetary damages, specific performance, and injunctive relief. Forcing a party to waive all other remedies besides injunctive relief and then hindering that relief with bond and damage showings, limits their recourse in a way that undermines the fundamental principles of contract law and makes enforcement unfairly burdensome.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: remedy | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you remedy A remedy is a form of court enforcement of a legal right resulting from a successful civil lawsuit . Remedies are designed to address the harm suffered by a party due to the unlawful actions or omissions of another party. They ensure that justice is served by restoring the injured party to their original position or by providing relief appropriate to the circumstances. Remedies fall into three general categories: Damages - Monetary compensation for the plaintiff ’s losses , injury , and/or pain or restitutionary measures designed to restore the plaintiff’s status to what it was prior to the violation of his or her rights, Coercive remedies - Requiring a party to do or omit doing a specific act through injunctive relief or a court order of specific performance (a court mandates that the party fulfill contractual obligations. See Contracts ). Declaratory judgment - The court determines individual rights in a specific situation without awarding damages or ordering particular action. Because of their historical origins, monetary damages are often referred to as a legal remedy while coercive and declaratory remedies are termed equitable remedies. Plaintiffs can also receive provisional remedies when a court uses its discretionary power to prevent harm to the plaintiff while the plaintiff’s rights are still being determined. Such remedies include temporary injunctions , attachment , and garnishment . [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex accidents and injuries LIFE EVENTS tort damages THE LEGAL PROCESS courts wex definitions courts and procedure Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction by forcing the Disclosing Party to waive all other rights or remedies besides injunctive relief, and then hindering that relief by requiring a bond and showing of actual monetary damages. This undermines the principles of contract law, as described in the Law Explanation, by limiting recourse in an unfairly burdensome way. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this, stating that remedies are designed to address harm suffered and ensure justice by restoring the injured party to their original position. By forcing a waiver of remedies and adding burdens to injunctive relief, the contract deviates from this principle, creating a misaligned terminology legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws of the State of Massachusetts and both parties further consent to jurisdiction by the state an d federal courts sitting in Boston , Massachusetts .
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws of the State of Delaware and both parties further consent to jurisdiction by the state an d federal courts sitting in Wilmington, Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law and jurisdiction to Delaware when one of the parties is a Massachusetts corporation introduces potential complications and contradictions. While parties can generally agree on a choice of law, the choice must have a reasonable relationship to the contract or the parties. If neither party has significant connections to Delaware, a court might disregard this clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 states that the law chosen by the parties to govern their contractual rights and duties will be applied if the particular issue is one which the parties could have explicitly resolved by a provision in their agreement directed to that issue. However, it also states the law of the state chosen by the parties will be applied, even if the issue could not have been resolved by explicit agreement in the contract, unless the chosen state has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction and there is no other reasonable basis for the parties' choice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Penn WebLogin Log in with your PennKey Username Password Log in Forgot username? Forgot password? Change my password Test my PennKey Privacy policy
perturbation.justification: The change in governing law and jurisdiction from Massachusetts to Delaware introduces a potential contradiction, as the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 states that the chosen state must have a substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction. The Perturbation Explanation suggests that if neither party has connections to Delaware, a court might disregard this clause. The changed text creates a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction by deviating from legally recognized requirements for choice of law, making the contract potentially unenforceable if Delaware has no connection to the parties.
